mnemonics.title: Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics
mnemonics.content: VISIBLE-HERO: Visibility of system status | Help users recognize errors | Error prevention | Recognition rather than recall | Consistency and standards | User control and freedom | Flexibility and efficiency of use | Aesthetic and minimalist design | Help and documentation | Match between system and real world

mnemonics.title: Remember Key UX Metrics
mnemonics.content: SCEN-TR: SUS | CSAT | Error Rate | NPS | Time on Task | Conversion Rate

acronyms.usage: 10-item questionnaire measuring perceived usability; score >68 = above average
Measures likelihood to recommend (0-10 scale); -100 to +100 range
Measures immediate post-interaction satisfaction (typically 1-5 or 1-10 scale)
Measures ease of task completion (1-7 scale); lower = easier
Overall experience of a user when interacting with a product or service

visualTips.visualization: Think of SUS as a thermometer: 0-50 = Cold/Bad, 50-68 = Room Temp/Average, 68-80 = Warm/Good, 80-100 = Hot/Excellent. The 68 mark is room temperature - below it needs improvement.
Picture a bucket: Promoters (9-10) pour positive water in, Detractors (0-6) drain it out, Passives (7-8) neither add nor drain. The NPS is the water level remaining.
Attitudinal = What users SAY (surveys, interviews). Behavioral = What users DO (clicks, time, paths). Think SAY vs DO.
Imagine 100 interactions as a jar. Each error is a drop. Error rate = drops / total fills × 100. More drops = higher rate.

analogies.analogy: UX metrics are like a car's dashboard - each gauge (metric) tells you something different about performance. Speed, fuel, temperature - you need multiple to understand the whole picture.
SUS is like a school grade - 68 is the passing/C average. Above 68 gets you into better 'usability' schools, below means you need remediation.
Bounce rate is like a shop where customers walk in and immediately walk out without browsing. High bounce = window display isn't appealing.
Think-aloud is like a tour guide narrating their thoughts while showing visitors around - you learn not just what they see, but how they interpret it.
A/B testing is like a science experiment - two hypotheses (versions), random groups, measurable outcomes. Only one 'wins' based on data.
